petitioner_background: The petitioner is a mathematical statistician seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. He obtained his first Ph.D. in 1989 from Moscow State University and a second Ph.D. in 2002 from Hebrew University. His career includes work at the Moscow Steel and Alloys Institute (1988-1990), 'Continent' Moscow Consulting (three years as senior scientific staff), teaching/research assistant at Hebrew University (1993-2000), visiting research associate at University of Southampton (summers 1997-1999) and University of Lincoln (spring 1999), and from 2001 working for User Technology Associates contracted to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) the Alex Berger Prize received in 2002 for his dissertation at Hebrew University, awarded for 'academic excellence'; (2) a certificate from the American Biographical Institute affirming 'honorary appointment' to the Research Board of Advisors on an unspecified date, with no documentation of its significance; (3) the Golda Meir Fellowship at Hebrew University (1995), described as 'a program to attract outstanding graduate and postgraduate scholars from around the world to pursue their research and studies in Israel,' with recipients ranging from lecturers to graduate students (petitioner listed in doctoral degree candidates section); and (4) a one-paragraph biography in edition five of '5,000 Personalities of the World.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that while Hebrew University may be nationally recognized, the issue is whether the prize or award itself is nationally recognized and recognizes excellence in the petitioner's field of endeavor. The Alex Berger prize recognized academic excellence, but academic study is not a field of endeavor—it is training for a future field of endeavor. Academic scholarships and student awards cannot be considered prizes or awards in the petitioner's field, and awards where competition is limited to other students cannot meet this criterion because experienced experts in the field do not compete for scholarships or Ph.D. fellowships. The AAO concurred with the director that the record lacks evidence of the significance of the American Biographical Institute, and inclusion in a biographic dictionary is not a prize or award. Counsel's argument that a 'combined review' of awards could establish national scope was rejected.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of membership in three associations: the American Statistical Association (ASA), the American Mathematical Society, and the Moscow Mathematical Society. The petitioner failed to submit evidence of the membership requirements for these associations, although the ASA membership application submitted by the petitioner suggested that ASA is merely a professional association requiring little more than payment of dues to join.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(ii) is clear and unambiguous—the petitioner must demonstrate membership in an association that requires outstanding achievements of its members. The record did not reflect that these associations require outstanding achievements of their general membership. Counsel's argument for a 'collective' review of multiple memberships was rejected as having no legal basis. The AAO noted that one of the petitioner's references, Dr. Phillip S. Kott, is a fellow of the ASA, suggesting a more exclusive membership category exists within ASA. Paying dues to join multiple professional associations is no more indicative of national or international acclaim than joining one, so multiple nonqualifying memberships are not comparable to the exclusive memberships required.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel asserted that citations of the petitioner's articles serve to meet this criterion, relying on a correspondence memorandum from Lawrence Weinig (July 30, 1992) for the proposition that a 'goodly number' of citations can suffice. Counsel also noted that the petitioner's biographic information appeared in '5,000 Personalities of the World.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that Mr. Weinig's memorandum stated 'a footnoted reference to the alien's work without evaluation . . . would be of little or no value' but that 'entries (particularly a goodly number) in a field . . . would more than likely be solid pieces of evidence.' However, Mr. Weinig did not identify the criterion to which this evidence would relate. The AAO held that the citation evidence is significant to the authorship of scholarly articles criterion, not this one. The statutory 'extensive evidence' requirement and the regulatory requirement to meet at least three criteria precludes considering citation records as evidence for three separate criteria. The plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii) requires that published material be about the petitioner—articles that cite his work are primarily about the author's work, not the footnoted materials. Additionally, appearing as one of thousands of successful individuals in a frequently published directory is not indicative of national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) evidence that he reviewed 13 articles in various journals for MathSciNet (Mathematical Reviews on the Web)—reviews which appeared to be of published articles, not typical peer-reviews of submitted manuscripts; (2) an e-mail advising the petitioner that a former professor, Danny Pfeffermann, recommended him as a potential reviewer for the Journal of the American Statistical Association; (3) a letter requesting his review of a manuscript for the Journal of Statistical Planning and Inference; (4) an e-mail from an editor at Biometrics thanking him for reviewing an article; (5) a letter addressed to Partha Lahiri requesting final review of a revised manuscript with a handwritten note from Mr. Lahiri to the original referee identified by the petitioner's first name (with the name suspiciously in original ink on an otherwise photocopied letter); and (6) evidence of teaching assistant duties at Hebrew University from 1993 to 2000. On appeal, the petitioner submitted materials from the websites of journals for which he served as a referee.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO acknowledged Mr. Weinig's statement that peer-review participation could be solid evidence but emphasized his conclusion that 'we expect the examiner to evaluate evidence, not simply count it.' Being recommended to review by a former professor is not indicative of national or international acclaim. Scientific and statistical journals are peer reviewed and rely on many professionals—peer review is routine in the field, and not every peer reviewer enjoys sustained acclaim. Without evidence setting the petitioner apart (such as reviewing an unusually large number of articles, receiving independent requests from a substantial number of journals, special solicitation from MathSciNet beyond a general call for volunteers, or serving in an editorial position for a distinguished journal), the review responsibilities do not meet this criterion. Regarding teaching assistant duties, duties inherent to one's occupation are generally not indicative of acclaim—it is inherent to teaching to review students' work, and not every teacher enjoys acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel referenced the petitioner's personal statement, curriculum vitae, publications, conference presentations, citation record, and reference letters from international individuals. Key letters included: (1) a joint letter from Professor V.V. Kalashnikov (Institute for System Studies), V.F. Matveyev (Moscow University), Professor V.V. Rykov (Gubkin's Institute of Oil and Gas), and S.N. Smirnov (Moscow University), who opposed or supervised his Moscow University thesis on 'the theory and applications of the coupling method'; (2) Professor Pfeffermann (thesis supervisor at Hebrew University) discussing the petitioner's 'new approach for inference from complex sample surveys' applied to Linear Regression Modeling, Generalized Linear Models, Population Totals and Small Area Estimation, and work on variance estimators for Seasonally Adjusted and Trend Estimators applied at Israel's Central Bureau of Statistics and the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics; (3) a professor emeritus at Carlton University who was external thesis examiner (authored 150 articles, advisory board member for U.S. Bureau of the Census and National Academy of Sciences); (4) two letters from the Bureau of Labor Statistics—the Associate Commissioner for the Office of Survey Methods Research and Dr. Dorfman (senior mathematical statistician who stated BLS 'constructed a one-man program for him'); (5) Dr. Smith (University of Southampton); (6) a distinguished professor at the University of Florida asserting the work 'will receive numerous citations in the years ahead'; (7) Dr. Kott (Chief Research Statistician at U.S. National Agricultural Statistics Service) who attempted to bring the petitioner to NASS; (8) a Principal Investigator at the Biostatistics Branch of the Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics of the National Cancer Institute; and (9) on appeal, a professor at Iowa University providing general praise.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concurred with the director that letters were mostly from the petitioner's circle of collaborators and did not establish the significance of his publication record. While major contributions are often published, concluding that meeting one criterion necessarily means meeting another would negate the requirement to meet at least three criteria. Letters from collaborators cannot by themselves establish acclaim—individuals with sustained acclaim should produce unsolicited materials reflecting that acclaim. Letters with mere assertions of widespread acclaim are less persuasive than letters providing specific examples of how contributions influenced the field. The joint Russian letter characterized the petitioner as competent but did not explain how his work influenced the field. Professor Pfeffermann noted that he, not the petitioner, was requested to write a book on the topic. The Carleton professor's accomplishments suggest the top level of the field is considerably higher than the petitioner's. Dr. Kott did not indicate NASS or ERS were considering adopting the techniques. The NCI investigator only stated findings 'have the potential of being implemented.' The Florida professor's statement that citations would come 'in the years ahead' suggested the petition was filed prematurely. Per Walters v. Metro. Educ. Enters. and Bailey v. U.S., 'major significance' must have meaning—contributions of major significance would be expected to have been investigated, confirmed, and applied by other experts. None of the references provided examples of how the petitioner's work was already influencing the field. The evidence demonstrated a talented statistician with potential but fell short of contributions of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that he has authored eleven published articles and presented his work at several conferences. The record contains evidence that 10 independent statisticians have cited the petitioner's work in the aggregate. The petitioner submitted six articles by independent statisticians reflecting two citations of his 1993 article, two citations of his 1990 article, one citation of his book chapter, and one citation of his 1999 article. The 1999 article is also cited in the introduction to a book in which he coauthored a chapter. Materials on appeal reflected no more than three citations for any one article or book chapter.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO cited the Association of American Universities' Committee on Postdoctoral Education Report and Recommendations (March 31, 1998), which considers publication 'expected' even among researchers who have not yet begun full-time academic/research careers. Publication of scholarly articles is not automatically evidence of sustained acclaim—the research community's reaction to those articles must be considered. With no more than three citations for any one of the petitioner's articles or book chapters, this number is not evidence that the petitioner's work is widely cited. Thus, the petitioner has not established that he meets this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel asserted that conference presentations could serve to meet this criterion, relying on Mr. Weinig's correspondence memorandum.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded this criterion relates to the visual arts. The AAO noted that while Mr. Weinig stated 'peer-reviewed presentations at academic symposia . . . would more than likely be solid pieces of evidence,' he did not indicate such presentations would constitute display at an artistic exhibition or showcase or comparable evidence. The inapplicability of this criterion to the petitioner's field is obvious from its plain language. The AAO consistently holds that conference presentations are much more comparable to publication of scholarly articles. Thus, conferences cannot meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed to have played a leading or critical role for the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, where he worked as a contractor through User Technology Associates beginning in 2001, assigned to the Office of Survey Methods Research.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The factors to consider under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(viii) are the nature of the position the petitioner was hired to fill and the reputation of the employer. While the Bureau of Labor Statistics may have a distinguished reputation, the AAO could not conclude that the petitioner, as a contractor for the Office of Survey Methods Research, was hired into a leading or critical role for the Bureau as a whole.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding that the petitioner failed to satisfy at least three of the regulatory criteria required to establish sustained national or international acclaim. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner distinguished himself as a statistician to such an extent that he achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence indicated that the petitioner shows talent as a statistician but was not persuasive that his achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field. Therefore, the petitioner did not establish eligibility pursuant to section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, and the burden of proof, which remains entirely with the petitioner under section 291 of the Act, was not sustained.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Walters v. Metro. Educ. Enters., 519 U.S. 202, 209 (1997)
Memorandum from Thomas Cook, Acting Associate Commissioner, Office of Programs, Significance of Letters Drafted by the Office of Adjudications (December 7, 2000)
July 30, 1992 correspondence memorandum from Lawrence Weinig, Assistant Commissioner
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted a suspicious evidentiary issue with one submission: a letter addressed to Partha Lahiri requesting final review of a revised manuscript contained a handwritten note from Mr. Lahiri to the original referee, identified only by the petitioner's first name. While the letter, signature, and handwritten note were photocopied, the petitioner's first name was in original ink, suggesting it was added at a later time—implying possible post-hoc alteration of the document to associate the petitioner with the review. Additionally, the AAO addressed a procedural argument by counsel relying on a 1992 correspondence memorandum from Lawrence Weinig, clarifying (per the December 7, 2000 Thomas Cook memorandum) that correspondence memoranda issued to a single individual do not constitute official CIS policy and are not binding on CIS officers.
